fields.name: CBC (Complete Blood Count)
fields.description: Evaluates overall health and detects wide range of disorders, including anemia and infection.
fields.aliases: Hemogram, Complete Blood Cell Count, CBC with Differential
fields.measures: Red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and red cell indices.
fields.identifies: Anemia, acute or chronic infections, immune deficiencies, clotting issues, and blood cancers.
fields.about: A Complete Blood Count (CBC) is a fundamental diagnostic blood test used to evaluate your overall health status. It measures the levels of various cellular components of the blood to detect broad physiological conditions.
fields.prep: No specific preparation or fasting is required. You can eat and drink normally before the blood sample collection.
fields.why: This test is routinely recommended as part of an annual checkup, to monitor ongoing treatments, or if you experience fatigue, fever, bruising, or weakness.
fields.interpretations: Low hemoglobin or RBC counts suggest anemia. Elevated white blood cell counts typically indicate an active infection or inflammation. Abnormal platelet levels can explain clotting or bleeding issues.

fields.name: HbA1c (Glycated Hemoglobin)
fields.description: Measures average blood sugar levels over the past 3 months to diagnose or monitor diabetes.
fields.aliases: A1c, Glycohemoglobin, Glycated Hemoglobin Test
fields.measures: Percentage of hemoglobin bonded with glucose molecules in the bloodstream over red blood cell lifespan.
fields.identifies: Prediabetes, Type 1 Diabetes, Type 2 Diabetes, and long-term blood glucose control quality.
fields.about: The HbA1c test reports your average blood glucose levels over the last 90 days. Because red blood cells live for about 3 months, this test provides a stable, long-term history of glucose control.
fields.prep: Fasting is not required for this test. You can have it done at any time of the day regardless of meals.
fields.why: Highly recommended for diagnosing diabetes or prediabetes, and for routine monitoring in diabetic patients to assess treatment efficacy.
fields.interpretations: Below 5.7% is Normal. 5.7% to 6.4% indicates Prediabetes. 6.5% or higher on two separate tests indicates Diabetes.

fields.name: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)
fields.description: Measures TSH levels to screen for and diagnose thyroid disorders like hypo/hyperthyroidism.
fields.aliases: Thyrotropin, Thyroid Stimulating Hormone Serum Test
fields.measures: TSH levels produced by the pituitary gland to regulate thyroid activity.
fields.identifies: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) and Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid).
fields.about: TSH tells your thyroid gland how much hormone to produce. When thyroid hormone levels drop, TSH goes up. If thyroid levels are high, TSH drops.
fields.prep: No fasting is required, but it is recommended to get the test in the morning as TSH levels fluctuate naturally throughout the day.
fields.why: Crucial if you show symptoms like unexplained weight gain or loss, severe fatigue, temperature sensitivity, or dry skin.
fields.interpretations: High TSH levels suggest an underactive thyroid (Hypothyroidism). Low TSH levels suggest an overactive thyroid (Hyperthyroidism).

fields.name: Lipid Profile
fields.description: Checks cholesterol and triglyceride levels to assess cardiovascular risk.
fields.aliases: Cholesterol Panel, Coronary Risk Profile, Lipid Panel
fields.measures: Total cholesterol, HDL (good) cholesterol, LDL (bad) cholesterol, VLDL, and triglycerides.
fields.identifies: Hypercholesterolemia, risk of heart attack, coronary artery disease, and stroke.
fields.about: A lipid profile measures lipids (fats) in your blood. High levels of LDL cholesterol can build up in arteries, leading to cardiovascular complications.
fields.prep: Fasting of 10 to 12 hours is mandatory before sample collection. Only water is permitted during this fasting period.
fields.why: Essential for evaluating heart health, monitoring cholesterol medication efficacy, and checking cardiovascular risk factors.
fields.interpretations: Optimal LDL is below 100 mg/dL. High HDL (above 60 mg/dL) is protective. High triglycerides (above 150 mg/dL) indicate increased metabolic risk.

fields.name: Kidney Function Test (KFT)
fields.description: Assesses renal health by measuring urea, creatinine, and uric acid.
fields.aliases: Renal Function Test (RFT), Kidney Panel
fields.measures: Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN), serum creatinine, uric acid, electrolytes (sodium, potassium, chloride).
fields.identifies: Kidney damage, acute renal failure, chronic kidney disease (CKD), and dehydration.
fields.about: The Kidney Function Test evaluates how well your kidneys are filtering wastes from your blood. Healthy kidneys clear creatinine and urea efficiently.
fields.prep: No specific fasting is required, but you should avoid eating large amounts of meat before the test.
fields.why: Recommended if you have hypertension, diabetes, kidney stones, or symptoms like swelling in feet and hands or changes in urination.
fields.interpretations: Elevated creatinine and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) indicate diminished kidney filtration capacity.

fields.name: Liver Function Test (LFT)
fields.description: Measures key enzymes and proteins to evaluate liver function and health.
fields.aliases: Hepatic Function Panel, Liver Panel
fields.measures: ALT, AST, ALP enzymes, total protein, albumin, globulin, and total/direct/indirect bilirubin.
fields.identifies: Hepatitis, liver cirrhosis, gallbladder obstruction, liver damage from drugs/alcohol.
fields.about: Liver Function Tests measure a variety of proteins and enzymes produced by liver tissue to screen for injury, infections, or inflammatory conditions.
fields.prep: No fasting is required, though certain medications may affect results. Inform your clinician about current prescriptions.
fields.why: Indicated if you experience abdominal pain, jaundice (yellowing of eyes/skin), nausea, or if you regularly consume alcohol.
fields.interpretations: Elevated ALT and AST enzymes suggest active liver injury. Higher bilirubin levels indicate bile duct issues or liver dysfunction.

fields.name: Vitamin D (25-Hydroxy)
fields.description: Determines vitamin D levels to check for deficiency and support bone health.
fields.aliases: 25-OH Vitamin D, Calcidiol Test
fields.measures: Concentration of 25-hydroxyvitamin D in the bloodstream.
fields.identifies: Vitamin D deficiency, osteoporosis risk, bone weakness, and calcium absorption issues.
fields.about: Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption, maintaining bone density, and regulating immune functions. This test determines your body's vitamin D status.
fields.prep: No fasting is required for Vitamin D testing.
fields.why: Recommended if you experience muscle aches, bone pain, joint stiffness, or if you have limited sunlight exposure.
fields.interpretations: Levels below 20 ng/mL indicate deficiency. 20-30 ng/mL is considered insufficient. Over 30 ng/mL is optimal.

fields.name: Vitamin B12
fields.description: Measures B12 levels which are crucial for nerve function and red blood cell production.
fields.aliases: Cobalamin Test, Serum Vitamin B12
fields.measures: Vitamin B12 concentration in blood serum.
fields.identifies: B12 deficiency, pernicious anemia, neurological dysfunction, and cognitive changes.
fields.about: Vitamin B12 is essential for brain health, nerve tissue development, and red blood cell synthesis. A deficiency can cause permanent nerve damage if untreated.
fields.prep: No fasting is required, but avoid taking B12 supplements for 24 hours prior to the blood draw.
fields.why: Crucial for vegetarians/vegans, older adults, or anyone showing signs of tingling in limbs, memory loss, or chronic fatigue.
fields.interpretations: Normal range is 200 to 900 pg/mL. Levels below 200 pg/mL suggest a significant B12 deficiency.

fields.name: Urine Routine & Microscopy
fields.description: Analyzes urine characteristics to screen for UTIs, kidney disorders, and metabolic issues.
fields.aliases: Urinalysis, UA, Urine R/M
fields.measures: Physical appearance, chemical properties (pH, protein, glucose, ketones), and microscopic elements (RBCs, WBCs, crystals).
fields.identifies: Urinary tract infections (UTIs), kidney disease, diabetes (via glucosuria), and hydration status.
fields.about: A routine urinalysis screens for early signs of disease. It evaluates physical, chemical, and microscopic features of the urine sample.
fields.prep: Clean-catch midstream urine sample is preferred. No fasting is required.
fields.why: Highly useful for routine screening, prenatal checkups, pre-surgery evaluation, or if experiencing painful or frequent urination.
fields.interpretations: Presence of nitrites or leukocyte esterase suggests a UTI. Protein in urine suggests kidney issues. Glucose indicates potential diabetes.

fields.name: CBC (complete blood Count)
fields.aliases: Haemoglobin Test, Blood Test, Platelet Test, Complete blood examination, CBC, Complete Blood Count
fields.measures: Components of blood, e.g., RBCs, WBCs, platelets, hemoglobin
fields.identifies: Conditions like infections, anaemia, leukemia, and immune disorders
fields.about: The Complete Blood Count test is a standard blood test that helps in assessing your general health. This test helps monitor the different types of cells present in the blood, such as red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets, to diagnose health conditions. Additionally, it provides valuable information about the effects of medications or medical conditions on the body and helps assess the health of the immune system.
fields.prep: No special preparation needed. You can eat and drink normally before the test.
fields.why: To screen for, diagnose, or monitor a variety of health conditions. Typically ordered as part of a routine checkup or if you are feeling fatigued or having symptoms of infection.
fields.interpretations: High or low counts of RBC, WBC, or platelets can indicate an underlying medical condition requiring clinical evaluation by a medical practitioner.

fields.name: Comprehensive Health Check
fields.description: Comprehensive health checkup for general assessment
fields.aliases: Comprehensive Health Check Test, Standard Panel, General Diagnostics
fields.measures: Key physiological biomarkers associated with Full Body
fields.identifies: Underlying health conditions, deficiencies, and organ functions
fields.about: The Comprehensive Health Check is a comprehensive health check designed to evaluate essential functions. It helps detect early warning signs, monitors chronic issues, and guides personalized medical suggestions.
fields.prep: Fasting may be required depending on specific test parameters. Standard is 8-12 hours fasting.
fields.why: Important for routine health checkups, early detection of potential risks, and tracking general wellness over time.
fields.interpretations: All diagnostic reports should be discussed with a certified doctor or healthcare professional for accurate diagnosis and clinical advice.

fields.name: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)
fields.aliases: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone Test, T3, T4, TSH Profile, Thyroid Panel
fields.measures: Levels of TSH, T3, and T4 thyroid hormones in the blood
fields.identifies: Hypothyroidism, Hyperthyroidism, and other thyroid imbalances
fields.about: The thyroid panel evaluates thyroid gland activity. The thyroid gland produces hormones that control metabolic processes, including heart rate, calorie burning, and body temperature. TSH regulates thyroid hormone production.
fields.prep: No specific fasting required, but certain supplements or medications may affect results. Consult your physician.
fields.why: If you experience symptoms of thyroid dysfunction, such as unexplained weight gain or loss, fatigue, temperature sensitivity, or changes in heart rate.
fields.interpretations: High TSH usually suggests hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid), while low TSH suggests hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid).

fields.name: Diabetes Screening Package
fields.aliases: HbA1c Test, Fasting Blood Sugar, Glycated Hemoglobin, Blood Glucose Test, Sugar Profile
fields.measures: Average glucose levels in the blood over the last 2-3 months
fields.identifies: Diabetes, Prediabetes, and glucose fluctuations
fields.about: A diabetes test measures glucose levels or glycated hemoglobin in your blood to diagnose or monitor diabetes. Routine screening is vital for early detection, enabling timely lifestyle changes and medical management to prevent chronic complications.
fields.prep: Fasting for 8-10 hours is typically required for fasting blood glucose, though HbA1c does not require fasting.
fields.why: Ordered to screen for prediabetes or diabetes, especially if you have a family history, are overweight, or experience symptoms like increased thirst or frequent urination.
fields.interpretations: HbA1c levels below 5.7% are normal, 5.7% to 6.4% indicate prediabetes, and 6.5% or higher indicate diabetes.
